deepResearchReview.note: No Deep Research review file was present in docs/journey/checkpoints at generation time.

authoringStandard.shift: From questions about the curriculum to questions that make the learner think through what the model is doing.
bannedStems: Which phrase best describes the topic?
authoringStandard.distractorStrategy: Misconception-first, glossary-near distractors with stable represented terms and choice IDs.

summary.pilotScope: First six Journey learning cards only.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason that an LLM generates response tokens by using learned weights and current context, without implying awareness or perfect search.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish fluent generated behavior from conscious understanding.
Separate inference from training and search/database retrieval.
Connect prompt, response, token, weight, and inference in one model-centered trace.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Inference uses fixed learned weights and temporary context to score, sample, append, and repeat next tokens.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: A fluent LLM must be conscious.
An LLM is a database or search engine that knows every file.
A normal prompt durably teaches the model new weights.

draft-what-is-llm-q1.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

draft-what-is-llm-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which description best fits What Is an LLM in this learning card?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does What Is an LLM? most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

questions.question: When an LLM answers a prompt by generating one token at a time, what is the model doing at each step?

choices.text: Using learned weights and current context to score possible next tokens, choose one, append it, and run again.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.

choices.text: Reading the prompt with conscious understanding before writing a complete answer.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because fluent text can sound like a human explanation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because fluent text can sound like a human explanation.

choices.text: Searching all documents it has ever seen and copying the best passage.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A plain LLM generates from learned patterns and current context; retrieval is an added system step.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because people often equate broad model knowledge with search.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because people often equate broad model knowledge with search.

choices.text: Changing its weights every time the user sends a prompt.
choices.feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model appears to adapt within a conversation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the model appears to adapt within a conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners trace the actual score-sample-append loop instead of naming the card.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports the core badge distinction between inference, training, context, and next-token generation.

questions.question: A model gives a fluent explanation of a poem. What is the safest model-centered interpretation?

choices.text: It generated likely response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.

choices.text: It must have inner experience because the explanation sounds human.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The model can imitate explanation without being a human reasoner.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because human-like language invites human-like interpretation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because human-like language invites human-like interpretation.

choices.text: It looked up a hidden database record that contains the perfect interpretation.
choices.feedback: Close, but this describes retrieval or search better than a plain LLM forward pass.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because polished answers can feel copied from a stored source.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because polished answers can feel copied from a stored source.

choices.text: The prompt permanently taught the model a new literary skill during that chat.
choices.feedback: That would require training. In a normal chat, the prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.
choices.representedMisconception: prompt/context change vs durable weight update
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model may use earlier context in the same conversation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the model may use earlier context in the same conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners evaluate a real human interpretation trap: fluent output without inner awareness.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports demystification without hype or fear.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Use the AI family map to reason about what kind of generative system an LLM is and what it is not.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish AI, machine learning, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, and symbolic AI.
Avoid treating all AI or all generative AI as ChatGPT-like LLMs.
Recognize that different generative systems can produce different media through different mechanisms.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: LLMs are deep-learning generative AI systems focused on language/code; diffusion is a different generative family.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: All generative AI works like ChatGPT.
AI and machine learning are interchangeable labels.

draft-where-llms-fit-q1.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

draft-where-llms-fit-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which statement best explains Where LLMs Fit in this learning card?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does Where LLMs Fit most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

questions.question: A campus tool summarizes text with an LLM and another tool creates images by denoising noise. What does that comparison show?

choices.text: Both can be generative AI, but the LLM focuses on language/code while diffusion often generates by denoising media.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.

choices.text: Both tools are LLMs because all generative AI works like ChatGPT.
choices.feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because ChatGPT is many learners’ first example of generative AI.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because ChatGPT is many learners’ first example of generative AI.

choices.text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates text tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but diffusion models can be AI without using the LLM next-token text loop.
choices.representedMisconception: LLM output vs broader generative outputs
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this app starts with language models.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because this app starts with language models.

choices.text: Machine learning and AI mean exactly the same thing, so the categories do not matter.
choices.feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI landscape.
choices.representedMisconception: AI vs machine learning category boundary
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the terms are often used loosely in public discussion.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the terms are often used loosely in public discussion.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners compare two model families by mechanism and output type.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prevents overgeneralizing LLM lessons to all AI systems.

questions.question: When people say an LLM sits inside the broader AI family, which category map is most useful?

choices.text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; deep learning uses layered networks; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs generate language/code.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The map keeps overlapping terms useful without making them interchangeable.

choices.text: LLMs are the parent category that contains all AI systems.
choices.feedback: Not quite. LLMs are an important branch, not the root of the AI tree.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because LLMs currently dominate public attention.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because LLMs currently dominate public attention.

choices.text: Symbolic/rule-based AI and learned models are identical because both run on computers.
choices.feedback: Close, but rule-based systems rely on explicit rules while learned models use patterns shaped from data.
choices.representedMisconception: symbolic AI vs learned model boundary
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both can be packaged inside the same software product.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both can be packaged inside the same software product.

choices.text: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same next-token loop.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Diffusion often denoises patterns; LLM text generation is usually autoregressive.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both are generative AI.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both are generative AI.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners build a category map they can use later when mechanisms diverge.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports risk literacy by avoiding one-size-fits-all AI claims.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason through the difference between explicit rules and learned weights, while recognizing that real AI products may combine both.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish symbolic/rule-based systems from deep-learning systems.
Explain loss as the training signal that guides weight adjustment.
Avoid treating LLM fluency as consciousness or treating hybrid apps as pure learned models.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Deep learning learns from examples by predicting, measuring loss, and adjusting weights; symbolic AI relies on explicit rules.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Fluent model output proves conscious reasoning.
Any rule, filter, or retrieval step means there is no learned model.

draft-history-q1.rationale: The boundary is right, but the stem should ask the learner to compare actual system behavior.

draft-history-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-history-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which phrase best describes how LLMs use learned patterns rather than only hand-written rules?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The boundary is right, but the stem should ask the learner to compare actual system behavior.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does Rationalists vs Empiricists most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which common confusion does Rationalists vs Empiricists help learners avoid?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

questions.question: If a system solves a task only by following hand-written if-then rules, how is it different from an LLM?

choices.text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.

choices.text: The rule-based system updates weights from loss during every task.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss is nearby in the deep-learning explanation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because loss is nearby in the deep-learning explanation.

choices.text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
choices.feedback: That gives the LLM too much inner life. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because LLM language can sound like reasoning from the inside.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because LLM language can sound like reasoning from the inside.

choices.text: There is no difference; both are just fixed rulebooks.
choices.feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both systems can produce predictable outputs.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both systems can produce predictable outputs.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners compare two system designs rather than reciting intellectual history.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds the architecture boundary needed for later training/inference distinctions.

questions.question: During deep-learning training, why does the system measure loss?

choices.text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.

choices.text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss sounds like a judgment word.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because loss sounds like a judgment word.

choices.text: Loss is the probability distribution used during sampling.
choices.feedback: Close, but probability helps choose outputs during generation; loss guides weight updates during training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both involve numbers during model work.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both involve numbers during model work.

choices.text: Loss is a retrieved source that gets added to context.
choices.feedback: That describes retrieval better. Loss belongs to training, not context insertion.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because later systems can add outside text before generation.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because later systems can add outside text before generation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners identify the causal role of loss inside training.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Loss is needed for badge-worthy understanding of how weights change.

questions.question: A university AI app uses an LLM plus policy filters, retrieval, and hand-written rules. What should a model-literate user conclude?

choices.text: Modern systems can combine learned models with rules/tools; the LLM itself is still a learned pattern system.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Products can be hybrid even when the central model is learned from data.

choices.text: Any rule in the app means the LLM is no longer trained from data.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Rules can wrap or guide a learned model without erasing the learned weights inside it.
choices.representedMisconception: hybrid system vs pure symbolic replacement
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because rule-based pieces can be visible in product behavior.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because rule-based pieces can be visible in product behavior.

choices.text: Any fluent answer means the app is consciously reasoning.
choices.feedback: This choice gives fluency too much mind. The app can combine tools and still lack conscious understanding.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the whole app may feel conversational.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the whole app may feel conversational.

choices.text: Retrieval or filters permanently fine-tune the model on every use.
choices.feedback: Close, but retrieval and filters can shape the current response without durably changing model weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the app appears to adapt to local material.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the app appears to adapt to local material.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners reason about real product stacks, not just model families.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports institutional AI literacy: model, wrapper, tool, and policy are not the same thing.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Trace training as the durable weight-changing loop and separate it from ordinary inference, sampling, retrieval, and context changes.
secondaryObjectives: Identify loss and weight update as the learning step.
Explain why a normal chat does not usually update model weights.
Trace example, prediction, loss, and update as the durable training path.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Training uses examples and loss to update parameters; inference uses fixed weights and temporary activations.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Training is the same as sampling or generation.
Weight updates, context changes, and retrieved text are interchangeable.

draft-training-q1.rationale: The training/inference boundary is essential, but the pilot should ask about a concrete training moment.

draft-training-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-training-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

draft-training-q4.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: What makes training different from inference?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The training/inference boundary is essential, but the pilot should ask about a concrete training moment.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does Training most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which common confusion does Training help learners avoid?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: How does Training connect to the prompt's Journey?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

questions.question: During training, the model predicts a target token and gets it wrong. What makes that moment learning rather than ordinary inference?

choices.text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.

choices.text: The model merely appends the wrong token to a live response.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because generation also chooses and appends tokens.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because generation also chooses and appends tokens.

choices.text: The user prompt is stored as permanent memory.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Training updates weights; a prompt in context is temporary unless stored elsewhere.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because chat context can feel remembered.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because chat context can feel remembered.

choices.text: Softmax turns the wrong answer into truth.
choices.feedback: Close, but softmax turns scores into probabilities; it does not make an answer true or update weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because softmax appears in the later probability step.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because softmax appears in the later probability step.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners locate the precise causal step that changes the model.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Core badge distinction: training changes weights; inference uses them.

questions.question: A student asks a chatbot one question. Unless a separate training process runs, what usually does not happen?

choices.text: The model’s weights are durably updated by that chat.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: The model uses its fixed weights to process the context.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That usually does happen during inference; the question asks what does not usually happen.
choices.representedMisconception: confusing what does happen during inference with what does not
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this is part of ordinary model use.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because this is part of ordinary model use.

choices.text: Temporary hidden states form during the forward pass.
choices.feedback: Close, but temporary hidden states can form during inference. They are not durable weight updates.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because hidden states are internal model numbers.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because hidden states are internal model numbers.

choices.text: The model scores possible next response tokens.
choices.feedback: That is ordinary generation behavior. It is not the durable training step.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because token scoring is also internal model work.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because token scoring is also internal model work.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners reason from a familiar chat scenario to the durable/temporary boundary.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Reduces the myth that every user chat trains the base model.

questions.question: Why can training change how the model behaves tomorrow, while inference usually cannot?

choices.text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.

choices.text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both training and inference run model computations.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both training and inference run model computations.

choices.text: Training only changes the visible prompt text.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes the model’s learned numbers.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompt text clearly changes a response.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because prompt text clearly changes a response.

choices.text: Training guarantees every future answer is true.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because training sounds like making the model better.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because training sounds like making the model better.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners connect durability to parameters reused by future inference.

questions.question: In a training loop, which sequence is the durable learning path?

choices.text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.

choices.text: Prompt -> response -> append -> next response token.
choices.feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this is the model’s generation loop.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because this is the model’s generation loop.

choices.text: Search -> retrieved passage -> context -> answer.
choices.feedback: That describes RAG. It adds temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because retrieval can improve answers.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because retrieval can improve answers.

choices.text: Noise -> denoise -> image/video sample.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That is a diffusion-style generation story, not the training loop for weight updates.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because diffusion is another generative process.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because diffusion is another generative process.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners choose the path with the durable update, not another plausible AI sequence.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares learners to distinguish training, RAG, diffusion, and autoregression later.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason that pretraining is broad durable pattern learning from next-token prediction over large datasets, not perfect recall or live browsing.
secondaryObjectives: Connect next-token targets, loss, weight updates, and broad model capability.
Separate pretraining from RAG, fine-tuning, inference, and database lookup.
Explain how broad data can shape weights without installing conscious understanding.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Pretraining repeats the training loop at large scale, changing weights before normal use.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Pretraining gives perfect recall of every source.
Pretraining happens during each user chat.
Fine-tuning or RAG explains all broad model knowledge.

draft-pretraining-q1.rationale: The durable weight-change idea is correct, but the pilot should name the pretraining signal and model mechanism.

draft-pretraining-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-pretraining-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

draft-pretraining-q4.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: During pretraining, what changes?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The durable weight-change idea is correct, but the pilot should name the pretraining signal and model mechanism.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does Pretraining most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which common confusion does Pretraining help learners avoid?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: How does Pretraining connect to the prompt's Journey?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

questions.question: During pretraining, what signal teaches the model broad language patterns across many examples?

choices.text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.

choices.text: A user conversation at deployment permanently rewrites the model.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Pretraining happens before normal use; an ordinary chat usually does not rewrite weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model can seem to adapt inside a chat.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the model can seem to adapt inside a chat.

choices.text: A human writes every rule the model will follow.
choices.feedback: Close, but pretraining is learned from examples, not a complete rulebook written by hand.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because software often has hand-written rules.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because software often has hand-written rules.

choices.text: The model perfectly stores each source document for later copying.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Pretraining shapes weights; it is not perfect document storage.
choices.representedMisconception: pattern learning vs perfect source recall
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because source data influences the model.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because source data influences the model.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners identify the training signal that builds broad capability.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects next-token prediction to durable weight learning.

questions.question: Why can pretraining create broad capability without making the model a perfect library of its sources?

choices.text: It installs statistical patterns in weights, not exact conscious recall of every document.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Pretraining can shape useful patterns without becoming a perfect library.

choices.text: Pretraining gives the model access to every private file on demand.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Training data exposure is not the same as access to every private file.
choices.representedMisconception: training data vs universal file access
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because broad training data can sound like broad access.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because broad training data can sound like broad access.

choices.text: Pretraining is the same as RAG inserting a document into context.
choices.feedback: Close, but RAG supplies temporary context during inference; pretraining durably changes weights before use.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both can bring outside information into answers.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both can bring outside information into answers.

choices.text: Pretraining happens only after a user asks a question.
choices.feedback: Pretraining happens before deployment. The user prompt later uses the pretrained weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because learners see the model only during use.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because learners see the model only during use.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners reason about what weights can and cannot contain.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports nuanced memory, privacy, and retrieval literacy.

questions.question: If pretraining examples include many styles and task shapes, what can change inside the model?

choices.text: Weights can shift so future prompts activate more useful language/code patterns.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining changes reusable model structure before later prompts arrive.

choices.text: Only the current context window grows larger.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Context is temporary input; pretraining changes weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because context also affects output.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because context also affects output.

choices.text: Only the app’s button labels or interface change.
choices.feedback: This describes product UI changes, not the model’s learned parameters.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users meet models through apps.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because users meet models through apps.

choices.text: Only a citation list is attached to answers.
choices.feedback: Close, but citations are response/support features; pretraining changes learned model weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because citations can make answers look knowledgeable.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because citations can make answers look knowledgeable.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners connect broad examples to future behavior through weights.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds a model-centered explanation for broad capability.

questions.question: A base model seems to know facts from public text. What is the model-literate explanation?

choices.text: Pretraining shaped weights with patterns from data, so the model may generate fact-like text without retrieving the original page.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Pretraining can support fact-like generation without live source lookup.

choices.text: It must be browsing the web by itself for every answer.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Some systems browse or retrieve, but a base model can also generate from pretrained weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because web search is a familiar way to know facts.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because web search is a familiar way to know facts.

choices.text: It must consciously remember reading those pages.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. It has learned weights, not conscious recollection.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the output can sound like human memory.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because the output can sound like human memory.

choices.text: Fine-tuning alone explains all broad knowledge.
choices.feedback: Close, but fine-tuning is targeted shaping after broad pretraining; it does not usually explain the whole foundation.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because fine-tuning is another training phase.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because fine-tuning is another training phase.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners diagnose the common fact/source/retrieval confusion.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports source literacy without implying omniscience.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Diagnose when a model has fit training examples too narrowly and explain why held-out validation checks generalization.
secondaryObjectives: Separate training performance from performance on new examples.
Recognize when targeted training can become too narrow.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Overfitting installs brittle patterns into weights; generalization means learned patterns transfer to unseen examples.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Perfect training performance proves a model learned well.
Validation data is just more context.
Lower loss or memorization automatically guarantees truthful future answers.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q1.rationale: The overfitting sign is correct, but the pilot should use an applied train/validation scenario.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which is a sign of overfitting in Overfitting and Generalization?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The overfitting sign is correct, but the pilot should use an applied train/validation scenario.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which mechanism or distinction does Overfitting and Generalization most directly teach?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

existingV0277DraftQuestions.question: Which common confusion does Overfitting and Generalization help learners avoid?
existingV0277DraftQuestions.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

questions.question: A model gets every training example right but fails on new validation examples. What problem is showing up?

choices.text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.
choices.feedback: Good diagnosis. The held-out examples reveal whether the pattern transfers.

choices.text: Generalization: the model is working well on unseen cases.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Generalization means good performance on new examples; this scenario shows the opposite.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both terms describe training performance.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both terms describe training performance.

choices.text: RAG: the model retrieved too many documents.
choices.feedback: This is not a retrieval problem. The issue is poor transfer from training examples to validation examples.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because retrieval can affect answer quality.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because retrieval can affect answer quality.

choices.text: Inference: the model is simply generating one token at a time.
choices.feedback: Close, but inference is normal model use; overfitting is a training/evaluation failure mode.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because all later model use involves inference.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because all later model use involves inference.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners diagnose overfitting from train/validation behavior.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds evaluation literacy: success on seen examples is not enough.

questions.question: Why do model builders test on held-out validation data instead of only training examples?

choices.text: Held-out data checks whether learned patterns work on examples the model did not train on.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Validation is about transfer, not memorized training performance.

choices.text: Validation data is used only to expand the current context window.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Validation examples test the model; they are not just temporary prompt context.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both involve examples supplied to a system.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because both involve examples supplied to a system.

choices.text: Training examples alone prove the model will work everywhere.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals the exact overfitting risk. Seen examples are not enough evidence.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because high training accuracy feels reassuring.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because high training accuracy feels reassuring.

choices.text: A lower loss always proves every future answer is true.
choices.feedback: Close, but lower loss is not a truth guarantee, especially outside the tested distribution.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because lower loss often signals improvement.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because lower loss often signals improvement.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners connect evaluation design to model behavior on new cases.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports responsible interpretation of AI benchmarks and claims.

questions.question: If fine-tuning makes a model excellent at one narrow template but worse on varied prompts, which boundary matters?

choices.text: The model may have become too narrow; useful training should preserve generalization beyond the examples.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but it can also make behavior brittle if it overfits.

choices.text: This proves prompts permanently change weights during inference.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The scenario is about fine-tuning/training effects, not prompts rewriting weights during inference.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts can change output behavior.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because prompts can change output behavior.

choices.text: This proves a rule-based AI is always better than a learned model.
choices.feedback: This overgeneralizes. The issue is narrow training behavior, not proof that rules are always better.
choices.representedMisconception: overfitting diagnosis vs symbolic superiority claim
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because brittleness can make rules feel safer.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because brittleness can make rules feel safer.

choices.text: This proves overfitting is just the model refusing to answer.
choices.feedback: Close, but overfitting is about brittle transfer from training examples, not simply a refusal behavior.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because poor behavior can show up as frustrating outputs.
choices.temptingBecause: Tempting because poor behavior can show up as frustrating outputs.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: Learners apply overfitting/generalization to targeted adaptation.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects training choices to trustworthy human use.
authoringNotes.reviewStatus: review by Deep Research for whether fine-tuning belongs here or later
